news_summary: Advancements in scientific research, inspiring stories of resilience, and proactive measures to protect youth online highlight today's positive strides in human rights.
news_summary_fr: Les avancées de la recherche scientifique, les récits inspirants de résilience et les mesures proactives visant à protéger les jeunes en ligne mettent en lumière les progrès encourageants réalisés aujourd’hui en matière de droits de l’homme.
news_summary_es: Los avances en la investigación científica, las inspiradoras historias de resiliencia y las medidas proactivas para proteger a los jóvenes en Internet ponen de relieve los avances positivos que se han logrado hoy en día en materia de derechos humanos.

articles.title: How DNA in dirt is shaking up the study of human origins
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 24 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00879-0Researchers are pulling clues from genetic material in ice age soils and rewriting chapters of human history.
articles.content:
When he was a doctoral student at the University of Copenhagen, Eske Willerslev was a nobody. At least, that’s how it seemed to the budding evolutionary geneticist, who was unable to lay his hands on one of the few coveted fossils that might still contain traces of ancient DNA.

Who were the ancient Denisovans? Fossils reveal secrets about the mysterious humans

But frustration turned to inspiration one autumn day in 2000, when he saw a dog depositing its morning poo onto the ground. The droppings contain DNA, he thought, and perhaps, even after rain washes them away, some DNA might remain. And if it does stick around, the genetic material of long-dead animals might also persist in the environment. That would mean he could learn something about those creatures, even without getting access to priceless museum specimens.

Willerslev’s idea was ridiculed by his professors at the time. “I’ve never heard anything as stupid as this,” he recalls one of them remarking. But his hunch bore incredible fruit. In a 2003 paper in Science, he showed that plant and animal DNA could be recovered from a Siberian permafrost core that stretched back 400,000 years1.

Even in the warmer temperatures of a New Zealand cave, Willerslev identified DNA from the extinct emu-like moa (Euryapteryx curtus) in 600-year-old sediments. It was the first time that researchers had used sediment alone to identify long-dead complex organisms.

Two decades on, the study of ancient DNA from sediments has matured into one of the most exciting tools for studying the past, say researchers. Interest in soil DNA surged nearly ten years ago, when scientists found that human DNA could also be isolated from ancient sediments. Laboratories that had once focused on extracting genetic material from precious fossils are now turning their attention to dirt. Archaeologists, too, are re-examining soil collected decades ago, keen to discover more about the past using this modern technology.

The complex history archived in sediments is ripe for exploration, says Willerslev. And it is vast. In 2022, his team coaxed snippets of DNA from two-million-year-old permafrost sediments at the northern tip of Greenland, the oldest such genetic material so far2. “It is a huge new blue ocean” of possibilities, says Willerslev. “You have humans, you have animals, you have plants, you have the whole bloody ecosystem.”

“It’s really incredible how much molecular information you have in sediments,” says Matthias Meyer, a molecular biologist at the Max Planck Institute for Evolutionary Anthropology in Leipzig, Germany. “I think we’re really just sort of scratching the tip of the iceberg in terms of what’s possible,” he says.

According to Willerslev, whether a site has fossil remains could become irrelevant. “My expectation would be, we can almost drop the bones,” he says, “and just go to the dirt.”

Sediments in Denisova Cave have yielded fossil remains and DNA from ancient humans.Credit: Eddie Gerald/Alamy

Sedimentary DNA has been particularly influential in the study of ancient humans, revealing important clues about early members of our own species, as well as about Neanderthals (Homo neanderthalensis) and the mysterious Denisovans, of which very few bones have been found.

“Without sediment DNA,” says Pere Gelabert, a population geneticist at the University of Vienna, discovering those clues “would be impossible”.

But even as groundbreaking findings about this new type of evidence make headlines, some researchers are urging caution about whether enough care is being taken to ensure that the results are reliable.

Hitting paydirt

For 14 years, sedimentary ancient DNA — also known as sedaDNA — remained the preserve of palaeoecologists reconstructing what life on Earth looked like using lake cores and permafrost samples. But a watershed moment for the field came in 2017, when scientists successfully identified DNA belonging to ancient humans in ice age soils3.

“When you have a story about humans, this is where you get people [interested],” says Diyendo Massilani, a palaeogeneticist at the Yale School of Medicine in New Haven, Connecticut, who was not involved in the 2017 study. As soon as someone found human DNA, he says, “then everybody was like, ‘let’s do sediments for everything’”.

The problem is that ancient human DNA in soil is vanishingly rare compared with the DNA of soil microorganisms and other fauna. To improve their odds of finding human DNA in samples, researchers at the Max Planck Institute for Evolutionary Anthropology — including the Nobel-prizewinning founder of the palaeogenomics discipline, Svante Pääbo — developed probes that capture human sequences selectively3.

Siberia’s ancient ghost clan starts to surrender its secrets

The study found human DNA in places where human fossils have not been discovered. This has highlighted sedaDNA’s potential for extending the fossil record. At the Trou Al’Wesse cave in Belgium, for instance, sedaDNA results confirmed a long-held suspicion — based on characteristic stone tools — that Neanderthals had occupied the site. At Denisova Cave in Siberia, researchers found DNA from both Neanderthals and a sister lineage, the Denisovans, which was named after the cave.

Some of the DNA appeared in layers without fossils. In a more extensive study of roughly 700 permafrost sediment specimens from Denisova Cave, one sample from a deep layer indicated that Neanderthals had arrived at the site 170,000 years ago — 30,000 years earlier than fossil evidence suggested4. And even though no bones have been found so far, sedaDNA places early modern humans in the cave from around 45,000 years ago.

DNA also places Neanderthals in a layer that contains one type of stone tool, and Denisovans in a separate layer with another type, linking each to its potential maker. That connection is often difficult to establish otherwise. Meyer and others are optimistic that ancient DNA could one day identify the makers of tools at further sites, and even the artists responsible for cave paintings.

Sedimentary DNA has also helped to solve a mystery about a cave on the Tibetan Plateau, nearly 3,000 kilometres southeast of Denisova Cave. In 1980, a monk had discovered an ancient jawbone at this site — called the Baishiya Karst Cave. In 2019, Qiaomei Fu, a palaeogeneticist at the Institute of Vertebrate Paleontology and Paleoanthropology in Beijing, and her colleagues reported on the pioneering use of ancient proteins, revealing that the 160,000-year-old jawbone belonged to a Denisovan5.

At the Baishiya Karst Cave on the Tibetan Plateau, researchers identified DNA that confirmed Denisovans once lived in the region.Credit: HAN Yuanyuan

However, the approach was new and there were doubts about the jawbone’s provenance, because it had been removed from the cave so long ago. In 2020, Fu found Denisovan DNA in the cave sediments, confirming that these hominins had once occupied the site. It was the first incontrovertible evidence that the archaic people had lived outside Siberia6.

Going nuclear

Both Meyer and Fu used ‘molecular fish hooks’ designed to recover human DNA from mitochondria, the tiny power generators in cells. With thousands of copies per cell, mitochondrial DNA (mtDNA) is more abundant — and easier to find — than nuclear DNA is. However, the nuclear genome’s size — three billion letters versus just 16,000 for mtDNA — and its inheritance from both parents make it better for discerning how past human populations split and intermingled through history.

The information that nuclear DNA could provide intrigued population geneticist Benjamin Vernot, who is also at the Max Planck institute in Leipzig. He wondered whether he could fish nuclear DNA out of some of the samples that have yielded mtDNA.

To do this, Vernot designed a set of 1.6 million probes for sequences scattered across the human genome. These would bind to sequences from Neanderthals, Denisovans and early modern humans. They would also pick up the DNA of ancient humans whose genetics are unknown. “There’s always the possibility that there’s going to be Homo erectus DNA in your sample,” he says. “We wanted to be prepared just in case.”

The probes successfully pulled nuclear sequences from the dirt, but the greater challenge was gleaning meaning from the sparse data. “Our best sediment samples were still really, really shitty,” says Vernot. Of the 1.6 million genomic probes, good samples contained sequences for just 10,000.

It took Vernot some eight months to develop computational methods that could deal with such meagre data7. Using these methods, Vernot turned to sediments from Galería de las Estatuas, a cave in northern Spain where excavations had uncovered a single Neanderthal foot bone and characteristic stone tools, but no genetic information.

From the mtDNA data, Vernot could discern two distinct Neanderthal populations, with one fully replacing the other around 100,000 years ago. But from the nuclear DNA, he could tell which samples were from a single male or female, and which contained a mixture of DNA from several individuals.

Gelabert has coaxed nuclear sequences from cave sediments, too8. But instead of capturing human sequences with probes, he took a brute-force approach, using ‘shotgun’ sequencing to read through all of the DNA extracted from the soil.

Humanity’s oldest art is flaking away. Can scientists save it?

However, his latest work, presented in a preprint, suggests that this approach isn’t as useful for population genetics. In a head-to-head comparison, DNA capture using molecular probes yielded 32 times more sequences at informative genome locations than did the shotgun method9. For the shotgun sequencing in his comparison, he says, “the effort is insane”.

Gelabert says that the extra work required to obtain nuclear DNA from sediments means that the process will probably be reserved for special cases; for instance, if the sediments contain a lot of genetic material. In most cases, mtDNA will suffice, he says. With mtDNA, “we can already have ideas of how many different lineages are in our sample”, he says, and “we can even trace back differences of populations”.

Lingering clues
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant scientific breakthrough in extracting ancient human DNA from sediments, which is revolutionizing the study of human origins. This advancement has broad implications for understanding human history, evolution, and migration, benefiting the general public's knowledge of humanity's past. The story is focused on a single topic and provides detailed context about the discovery, methods, and its impact on archaeology and genetics.
articles.personality_title: Scientists unlock ancient human secrets hidden in soil DNA
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – For many years, studying ancient human history relied mostly on fossils. These are bones or teeth that can hold DNA, but fossils are rare and hard to find. A new method looks at DNA trapped in soil and sediments, even where no bones have been found, opening a fresh way to learn about our ancient ancestors.

**What happened** – Starting in 2000, researcher Eske Willerslev had the idea that DNA from long-dead animals and humans could survive in dirt. Despite skepticism, he and other scientists developed techniques to recover this DNA from ancient soils, some over two million years old. They found DNA from Neanderthals, Denisovans, and early modern humans in places like Siberia and Greenland. This DNA helps identify who lived where and when, even linking ancient humans to the tools they used.

**Impact** – This discovery changes how we study human origins. Instead of relying only on bones, scientists can now analyze sediment DNA to uncover details about ancient people and ecosystems. It has revealed human presence in places where no fossils exist and pushed back timelines by tens of thousands of years. The method also helps connect ancient humans to their culture and environment more clearly than before.

**What's next step** – Researchers are improving ways to capture more detailed DNA, including nuclear DNA, which tells us more about relationships between ancient humans. They plan to use these techniques to study more sites worldwide, re-examine old soil samples, and possibly identify who made ancient tools or cave paintings.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Extracting DNA from ancient soils is revolutionizing our understanding of human history by revealing who lived where and when, even without fossils.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques découvrent les secrets anciens de l’homme cachés dans l’ADN du sol
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Pendant longtemps, l’étude de l’histoire humaine ancienne dépendait surtout des fossiles. Ce sont des os ou des dents qui peuvent contenir de l’ADN, mais les fossiles sont rares et difficiles à trouver. Une nouvelle méthode analyse l’ADN piégé dans la terre et les sédiments, même là où aucun os n’a été découvert, offrant une nouvelle façon d’en apprendre davantage sur nos ancêtres.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Dès 2000, le chercheur Eske Willerslev a eu l’idée que l’ADN d’animaux et d’humains morts depuis longtemps pouvait survivre dans la terre. Malgré le scepticisme, lui et d’autres scientifiques ont mis au point des techniques pour extraire cet ADN dans des sols anciens, certains vieux de plus de deux millions d’années. Ils ont trouvé de l’ADN de Néandertaliens, de Denisoviens et d’humains modernes anciens dans des endroits comme la Sibérie et le Groenland. Cet ADN aide à savoir qui vivait où et quand, même en reliant les humains anciens aux outils qu’ils utilisaient.

**Impact** – Cette découverte change la façon d’étudier les origines humaines. Au lieu de se fier uniquement aux os, les scientifiques peuvent maintenant analyser l’ADN des sédiments pour découvrir des détails sur les personnes anciennes et leurs environnements. Cela a révélé la présence humaine dans des lieux sans fossiles et repoussé les dates de présence de dizaines de milliers d’années. La méthode aide aussi à mieux comprendre la culture et l’environnement des anciens humains.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs améliorent les techniques pour capturer plus d’ADN détaillé, notamment l’ADN nucléaire, qui donne plus d’informations sur les relations entre anciens humains. Ils prévoient d’utiliser ces méthodes pour étudier plus de sites dans le monde, réexaminer d’anciens échantillons de sol et peut-être identifier qui a fabriqué des outils anciens ou des peintures rupestres.

**Résumé en une phrase** – L’extraction d’ADN dans les sols anciens révolutionne notre compréhension de l’histoire humaine en révélant qui vivait où et quand, même sans fossiles.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos revelan secretos antiguos del hombre ocultos en el ADN del suelo
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante mucho tiempo, el estudio de la historia humana antigua dependió principalmente de los fósiles. Estos son huesos o dientes que pueden contener ADN, pero los fósiles son raros y difíciles de encontrar. Un nuevo método analiza el ADN atrapado en la tierra y los sedimentos, incluso donde no hay huesos, abriendo una forma nueva de aprender sobre nuestros antiguos antepasados.

**Qué pasó** – A partir del año 2000, el investigador Eske Willerslev tuvo la idea de que el ADN de animales y humanos muertos hace mucho tiempo podría sobrevivir en la tierra. A pesar del escepticismo, él y otros científicos desarrollaron técnicas para recuperar este ADN de suelos antiguos, algunos de más de dos millones de años. Encontraron ADN de neandertales, denisovanos y humanos modernos antiguos en lugares como Siberia y Groenlandia. Este ADN ayuda a saber quién vivió dónde y cuándo, incluso relacionando a los humanos antiguos con las herramientas que usaban.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento cambia cómo estudiamos los orígenes humanos. En lugar de depender solo de huesos, los científicos ahora pueden analizar el ADN de los sedimentos para descubrir detalles sobre las personas antiguas y sus ecosistemas. Ha revelado la presencia humana en lugares sin fósiles y ha adelantado las fechas en decenas de miles de años. El método también ayuda a conectar a los humanos antiguos con su cultura y ambiente de manera más clara.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores están mejorando las formas de capturar ADN más detallado, incluido el ADN nuclear, que nos dice más sobre las relaciones entre humanos antiguos. Planean usar estas técnicas para estudiar más sitios en todo el mundo, reexaminar muestras antiguas de suelo y posiblemente identificar quién hizo herramientas antiguas o pinturas rupestres.

**Resumen en una frase** – Extraer ADN de suelos antiguos está revolucionando nuestra comprensión de la historia humana al revelar quién vivió dónde y cuándo, incluso sin fósiles.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of ancient layered cave sediments glowing softly from within, revealing delicate strands of DNA spiraling gently through the earth, intertwined with faint, ghostly silhouettes of mysterious hominin skull shapes and ancient stone tools embedded in the soil, all rendered in natural, earthy tones of ochre, brown, and muted greens.

articles.title: Rebuilding from the Thailand-Myanmar border: Thapyay’s journey of courage and new beginnings
articles.summary: “Struggling in these conditions is exhausting physically and mentally. But please do not give up. Protect your mental strength.”
articles.content:
On the Thai-Myanmar border, many lives are quietly being rebuilt.

Since the military junta launched a coup against the Myanmar government in February 2021, an estimated 3.7 million people have been internally displaced, according to data from the UN Refugee Agency (UNHCR). However, behind every story of displacement is a person learning to begin again, often with uncertainty, limited opportunities, and the weight of everything left behind. This is the story of Thapyay, one of many Myanmar women living in exile who continues to move forward with courage and determination.

Before the coup, she was a university lecturer and Associate Professor in Central Myanmar, dedicating more than 20 years to teaching. Her classroom was a place where young people learned, questioned, and imagined their futures.

But when the military coup happened in Myanmar, Thapyay joined the Civil Disobedience Movement (CDM), a group of young people fighting back against the junta regime through acts of civil disobedience and resistance. Like many CDM participants, the consequences came quickly. Remaining inside the country was no longer safe. At the end of 2022, Thapyay and her family crossed the border into Mae Sot, Thailand, seeking safety.

For someone who spent decades in academia, the transition was painful. But instead of trying to recreate her past, she began exploring new possibilities for the future.

Thapyay joined the Zin Yaw Women Rising Program, supported by Exile Hub and partners. The program offered career coaching, digital skills training, and peer support, with courses delivered through an online learning platform.

For Thapyay, the experience was transformative. She explains that “The most helpful part was not only the technical skills. It was learning to see myself differently again.”

She added, “It helped me understand what parts of my identity I needed to let go of and what parts I should hold onto with confidence.”

A new chapter

Today, Thapyay works as a content writer, allowing her to work safely from home. For her, this milestone represents far more than a job. It represents the possibility of rebuilding life after disruption.

“It was not easy to strip away my past titles and start again, but I now see this not as an ending but as the beginning of a new life.”

Her confidence, slowly but surely, is returning.

“For that, I am deeply grateful to the Zin Yaw program, the trainers, and the organizations that made this opportunity possible.”

Words for other women at the border

Thapyay knows that many other women in exile are facing similar challenges. Her message to them is simple, but powerful:

“Struggling in these conditions is exhausting physically and mentally. But please do not give up. Protect your mental strength.”

For many people rebuilding their lives after displacement, the future can feel uncertain. But Thapyay has learned something important along the way.

She encourages fellow women in exile, “Try to trust the process. Sometimes we focus too much on the result and forget that every small step we take today has value. One day, opportunities will appear. These struggles are simply part of the path forward.”

She added, “Circumstances may limit you, but no one can limit your talent or your effort.”

The skills she is learning, the work she is doing, and the resilience she continues to build are all part of that process. Along with many women living lives on the border, Thapyay continues walking that path, one step at a time.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the inspiring story of a displaced Myanmar woman rebuilding her life through education, skill development, and resilience after fleeing political violence. It reflects broader issues of displacement and empowerment of refugees, with tangible positive impact on individuals and communities affected by conflict. The story is focused, detailed, and significant for many people facing similar hardships.
articles.personality_title: From displacement to new skills: A Myanmar woman’s journey to rebuild life on the Thailand border
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Since a military coup in Myanmar in February 2021, about 3.7 million people have been forced to leave their homes but remain inside the country. Many have crossed into Thailand seeking safety. Among them are women like Thapyay, who face the challenge of starting over far from home.

**What happened** – Thapyay was a university professor in Myanmar for over 20 years before she joined a civil protest movement after the coup. Due to safety concerns, she fled with her family to Mae Sot, Thailand, at the end of 2022. There, she joined the Zin Yaw Women Rising Program, which helps displaced women learn new skills like digital work and offers support through online courses.

**Impact** – This program helped Thapyay not only gain new job skills but also change how she views herself after losing her old life. She now works as a content writer from home, showing that it is possible to rebuild a career and confidence even after big setbacks. Her story gives hope to others in exile facing tough challenges.

**What's next step** – Thapyay plans to keep developing her skills and encourages other displaced women to keep moving forward step by step. The program continues to support more women like her, helping them find ways to build new lives despite difficult circumstances.

**One-sentence takeaway** – After fleeing conflict, a former Myanmar professor found new strength and skills through training programs, showing how displaced women can rebuild their lives one step at a time.
articles.personality_title_fr: De la fuite à de nouvelles compétences : le parcours d’une femme birmane pour reconstruire sa vie à la frontière thaïlandaise
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Depuis un coup d’État militaire en Birmanie en février 2021, environ 3,7 millions de personnes ont dû quitter leur domicile mais restent dans le pays. Beaucoup ont traversé la frontière pour la Thaïlande à la recherche de sécurité. Parmi elles, des femmes comme Thapyay font face au défi de tout recommencer loin de chez elles.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Thapyay était professeure d’université en Birmanie pendant plus de 20 ans avant de rejoindre un mouvement de protestation civile après le coup d’État. Pour des raisons de sécurité, elle a fui avec sa famille à Mae Sot, en Thaïlande, fin 2022. Là, elle a rejoint le programme Zin Yaw Women Rising, qui aide les femmes déplacées à apprendre de nouvelles compétences, comme le travail numérique, et offre un soutien via des cours en ligne.

**Impact** – Ce programme a aidé Thapyay non seulement à acquérir de nouvelles compétences professionnelles, mais aussi à changer son regard sur elle-même après avoir perdu sa vie d’avant. Elle travaille maintenant comme rédactrice de contenu depuis chez elle, montrant qu’il est possible de reconstruire une carrière et la confiance malgré de grandes difficultés. Son histoire donne de l’espoir aux autres femmes en exil.

**Prochaine étape** – Thapyay prévoit de continuer à développer ses compétences et encourage les autres femmes déplacées à avancer pas à pas. Le programme continue de soutenir d’autres femmes comme elle, leur offrant des moyens de construire une nouvelle vie malgré les circonstances difficiles.

**Message clé en une phrase** – Après avoir fui le conflit, une ancienne professeure birmane a retrouvé force et compétences grâce à un programme de formation, montrant que les femmes déplacées peuvent reconstruire leur vie étape par étape.
articles.personality_title_es: De la expulsión a nuevas habilidades: la historia de una mujer de Myanmar reconstruyendo su vida en la frontera con Tailandia
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Desde un golpe militar en Myanmar en febrero de 2021, unas 3.7 millones de personas han tenido que abandonar sus hogares pero permanecen dentro del país. Muchas han cruzado a Tailandia buscando seguridad. Entre ellas están mujeres como Thapyay, que enfrentan el reto de comenzar de nuevo lejos de casa.

**Qué pasó** – Thapyay fue profesora universitaria en Myanmar por más de 20 años antes de unirse a un movimiento civil tras el golpe. Por razones de seguridad, huyó con su familia a Mae Sot, Tailandia, a finales de 2022. Allí, se unió al programa Zin Yaw Women Rising, que ayuda a mujeres desplazadas a aprender nuevas habilidades digitales y ofrece apoyo mediante cursos en línea.

**Impacto** – Este programa ayudó a Thapyay no solo a adquirir nuevas habilidades laborales, sino también a cambiar cómo se ve a sí misma después de perder su vida anterior. Ahora trabaja como redactora de contenido desde su casa, demostrando que es posible reconstruir una carrera y confianza tras grandes dificultades. Su historia da esperanza a otras mujeres en exilio.

**Próximo paso** – Thapyay planea seguir desarrollando sus habilidades y anima a otras mujeres desplazadas a continuar avanzando paso a paso. El programa sigue apoyando a más mujeres como ella, ayudándolas a construir nuevas vidas a pesar de las circunstancias difíciles.

**Frase clave** – Tras huir del conflicto, una exprofesora de Myanmar encontró nueva fuerza y habilidades mediante programas de formación, demostrando que las mujeres desplazadas pueden reconstruir sus vidas paso a paso.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a woman’s silhouette made of softly glowing pages of an open book, seated at a simple wooden desk beside a gently flowing river marking a border line, with a delicate thread of light connecting her to a distant, rising sun beyond misty mountains—symbolizing courage, learning, new beginnings, and resilience amid displacement.

articles.title: Social media bans and digital curfews to be trialled on UK teenagers
articles.summary: The government will interview the young people and their parents before and after they try the limits to assess their impact.
articles.content: The test, led by the UK government, will see 300 teens involved have their social apps disabled entirely, blocked overnight or capped to one hour's use - with some also seeing no such changes at all - in order to compare their experiences.
articles.reasoning: The UK government's trial of social media bans and digital curfews for teenagers aims to assess the impact on young people's wellbeing, representing a proactive policy effort with potential broad benefits for youth mental health and digital wellbeing.
articles.personality_title: UK begins trial to limit teenagers' social media use for better wellbeing
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many teenagers spend a lot of time on social media, and there are concerns about how this affects their mental health and sleep.

**What happened** – The UK government started a test involving 300 teenagers. Some will have their social media apps completely turned off, some will have access blocked overnight, others will be allowed only one hour of use each day, and some will continue using apps as usual. Researchers will talk with the teens and their parents before and after the trial to see how these changes affect them.

**Impact** – This trial is important because it is one of the first to directly test how different limits on social media affect young people’s wellbeing. It can provide clear evidence about whether reducing screen time helps teens feel better or sleep more.

**What's next step** – After the trial, the government will study the results to decide if social media limits should be recommended or introduced more widely. The findings could influence future rules about digital use for young people.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The UK is testing new ways to limit teenagers’ social media use to better understand how it affects their health and daily lives.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le Royaume-Uni lance une expérimentation pour limiter l’usage des réseaux sociaux chez les adolescents
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Beaucoup d’adolescents passent beaucoup de temps sur les réseaux sociaux, ce qui soulève des inquiétudes sur leur santé mentale et leur sommeil.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le gouvernement britannique a lancé une expérimentation avec 300 adolescents. Certains verront leurs applications de réseaux sociaux complètement désactivées, d’autres auront l’accès bloqué la nuit, certains seront limités à une heure d’utilisation par jour, et d’autres continueront normalement. Les chercheurs parleront aux adolescents et à leurs parents avant et après pour voir l’effet de ces changements.

**Impact** – Cette expérimentation est importante car elle teste directement comment différentes limites sur les réseaux sociaux influencent le bien-être des jeunes. Elle peut montrer clairement si réduire le temps d’écran aide les adolescents à se sentir mieux ou à mieux dormir.

**Prochaine étape** – Après l’expérimentation, le gouvernement analysera les résultats pour décider si des limites sur les réseaux sociaux doivent être recommandées ou appliquées plus largement. Ces résultats pourraient influencer les règles futures sur l’usage du numérique chez les jeunes.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le Royaume-Uni teste de nouvelles façons de limiter l’usage des réseaux sociaux chez les adolescents pour mieux comprendre leur impact sur la santé et la vie quotidienne.
articles.personality_title_es: Reino Unido inicia prueba para limitar el uso de redes sociales en adolescentes
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchos adolescentes pasan mucho tiempo en redes sociales, lo que preocupa por cómo afecta su salud mental y sueño.

**Qué pasó** – El gobierno del Reino Unido comenzó una prueba con 300 adolescentes. A algunos se les desactivarán completamente las aplicaciones de redes sociales, a otros se les bloqueará el acceso durante la noche, algunos tendrán solo una hora de uso diario, y otros seguirán usando las aplicaciones normalmente. Los investigadores entrevistarán a los jóvenes y a sus padres antes y después para ver cómo les afectan estos cambios.

**Impacto** – Esta prueba es importante porque examina directamente cómo diferentes límites en las redes sociales afectan el bienestar de los jóvenes. Puede mostrar claramente si reducir el tiempo frente a la pantalla ayuda a los adolescentes a sentirse mejor o a dormir mejor.

**Próximo paso** – Después de la prueba, el gobierno estudiará los resultados para decidir si se deben recomendar o implementar límites en el uso de redes sociales de forma más amplia. Los hallazgos podrían influir en futuras reglas sobre el uso digital en jóvenes.

**Conclusión en una frase** – El Reino Unido está probando nuevas formas de limitar el uso de redes sociales en adolescentes para entender mejor cómo afecta su salud y vida diaria.
